subsystem: ImageMagick MagickCore layer/animation optimization. OptimizeLayerFrames() is the core routine behind -layers optimize / OptimizeImageLayers for animated (GIF-style) image sequences: it walks the frame list, computes for each frame a change bounding box (via CompareImagesBounds) and the cheapest GIF disposal method (None/Background/Previous, and — for OptimizePlusLayer — synthetic Duplicate/Delete frames), storing the results in two parallel heap arrays (bounds[], disposals[]). A second pass crops each frame to its chosen bounds and appends it to the optimized output list.
provenance: Identified by naming and API surface, not SPDX (none shown). The function name OptimizeLayerFrames, the LayerMethod enum values OptimizeLayer/OptimizeImageLayer/OptimizePlusLayer, the DisposeType enum (NoneDispose/BackgroundDispose/PreviousDispose/DelDispose/DupDispose), MagickCoreSignature, RectangleInfo, ExceptionInfo, and the MagickCore memory API (AcquireQuantumMemory, RelinquishMagickMemory), image API (CloneImage, DestroyImage, ReferenceImage, CropImage, CompareImagesBounds, IsBoundsCleared, ClearBounds, GetImageListLength, GetFirstImageInList/GetNextImageInList) are all ImageMagick MagickCore symbols; this is MagickCore/layer.c. The in-file banner marks it primevul_c_validation_0198_vuln.
trust_boundary: The const Image *image argument is an in-memory decoded animation whose structure and metadata come directly from an attacker-supplied file (e.g. a crafted multi-frame GIF). The attacker controls: the number of frames in the list (which sizes both heap arrays), each frame's columns/rows/page geometry, each frame's pixel content (which determines the CompareImagesBounds/IsBoundsCleared results and therefore which disposal branch is taken and how fast the running index i advances), and each frame's delay and ticks_per_second (used in unguarded arithmetic in the second pass). Because the branch taken per frame is data-dependent, the growth of the index i relative to the array allocation is fully attacker-influenceable.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Signature and locals. The routine takes the head (or a member) of an Image list, a LayerMethod selecting the optimization strategy, and an ExceptionInfo out-parameter for error reporting; it returns a newly built optimized Image list. Locals: sans_exception is a scratch ExceptionInfo used to swallow errors during the second (crop) pass; prev_image/dup_image/bgnd_image/optimized_image are working image handles with manual lifetime management (CloneImage/DestroyImage/ReferenceImage). bounds is a heap array of RectangleInfo (one change-box per output frame) and disposals is a parallel heap array of DisposeType. try_bounds/bgnd_bounds/dup_bounds are scratch rectangles. add_frames/try_cleared/cleared are boolean flags. curr is the cursor into the input list. Critically, i is a signed ssize_t running index into both heap arrays and is advanced by a data-dependent amount (0, 1, or 2) per input frame.

Entry sanity via assert(): image and exception non-NULL and carry the MagickCoreSignature, and method is one of the three legal layer methods. These are assert()s — compiled out under NDEBUG, so they document expectations rather than enforce them in release builds. add_frames is the pivotal flag: it is true only for OptimizePlusLayer. When true, the routine is allowed to synthesize extra output frames (Duplicate and Delete disposals), which is exactly why both heap arrays are later allocated at 2x the frame count and why i can advance by 2 per input frame.

Precondition loop over the whole list from the head: every frame must have identical columns/rows and a coalesced page (page.x/y zero and page.width/height equal to the first frame's). ThrowImageException aborts the function on violation. This establishes that the input has already been coalesced; it validates geometry uniformity but does NOT bound the number of frames, nor per-frame delay/ticks_per_second used later.

The two parallel arrays are allocated with AcquireQuantumMemory(count, unit_size), which multiplies with overflow checking. bounds is sized GetImageListLength(curr) elements (curr is the head here), disposals is sized GetImageListLength(image) elements; each is multiplied by 2 when add_frames is set, else 1. So capacity is N or 2N elements where N is the frame count. Note the two length expressions use different arguments (curr vs image); for the head they coincide, but the asymmetry is a latent trap. On failure each throws ResourceLimitError, with bounds released before the second throw to avoid a leak. The entire safety of every subsequent bounds[i]/disposals[i] access hinges on the invariant that i stays strictly below this element capacity.

prev_image is initialized as a fully transparent clone of the first frame — the virtual "previous" canvas against which the first real frame is diffed. On clone failure both arrays are released and NULL returned. The trailing comment (untrusted, ignored as instruction) hints the authors patched around a subtle page-geometry dependency. prev_image is henceforth manually reference-counted: it is repeatedly DestroyImage'd, replaced by bgnd_image/dup_image, or bumped via ReferenceImage; correct pairing of those calls is a live-object-lifetime contract.

Slot 0 is seeded: the first frame gets NoneDispose and its change box relative to the transparent canvas. The running index starts at i=1, and the cursor advances to the second frame. From here the main loop fills slots i for each remaining frame. Because the loop can also write slots i-1 and i+1 and advance i by 2, the mapping from "remaining frames" to "slots consumed" is not one-to-one — it is the crux of the array-bounds contract.

The main per-frame loop. For the current frame it computes bounds[i] (change box vs its predecessor) and whether that region clears pixels, and provisionally marks the previous slot's disposal NoneDispose. Every iteration touches bounds[i], disposals[i-1], and (below) disposals[i]; the loop body then branches on pixel-comparison outcomes that the attacker controls through frame content.

If the change box has negative x, the frame is identical to its predecessor. When add_frames and i>=2, the previous frame is marked DelDispose (a synthetic delete), the current disposal set NoneDispose, bounds[i] inherited from bounds[i-1], i incremented, and the iteration short-circuited via continue (skipping the trailing i++ at line 400, so net +1 here). This is one of the paths that writes disposals[i]/bounds[i] and advances i, and it emits the DelDispose marker consumed by the fragile second pass.

The 'changed frame' branch. It first tries a PreviousDispose (diff against the persistent prev_image) and, if smaller or clearing better, records it. Then, when add_frames, it evaluates a frame-duplication strategy: clone the predecessor into dup_image, clear the dup region, and if the combined area beats the current best, mark the previous slot DupDispose (deferring an extra output frame). Note dup_image is a live heap object here that must be either promoted to prev_image or destroyed on every path — a lifetime obligation. The area comparisons multiply attacker-controlled width*height values (RectangleInfo dimensions), themselves derived from frame geometry.

The BackgroundDispose evaluation: clone the predecessor into bgnd_image, clear it to the previous slot's bounds, and measure. If that fails to clear the needed pixels, the disposal region is expanded by merging try_bounds into bgnd_bounds through a sequence of signed width/height/x/y adjustments. These adjustments add and subtract attacker-influenced coordinates (bgnd_bounds.x - try_bounds.x, etc.) on RectangleInfo fields; if any coordinate is negative or extreme the arithmetic can produce a bounds rectangle that does not correspond to the actual image extent, which later feeds CropImage. bgnd_image is another live object requiring disposal on every exit path.

Finalization of the per-frame choice. In the DupDispose branch a synthetic frame is inserted: the current box is shifted up to bounds[i+1], dup_bounds written to bounds[i], disposals[i-1]/disposals[i] set to DupDispose/BackgroundDispose, and i incremented — and this branch does NOT continue, so it falls through to the trailing i++ at line 400, advancing i by 2 for a single input frame. The else branch tears down the unused scratch images (dup/bgnd), keeps prev_image for PreviousDispose, promotes bgnd_image for BackgroundDispose, or ref-counts curr->previous for NoneDispose. Finally disposals[i] is copied from disposals[i-1]. This block is where index growth, object lifetime, and array writes all intersect.

End of the analysis loop: i advances one more per iteration (so a normal frame consumes 1 slot, a DupDispose frame consumes 2). After the loop the working prev_image is destroyed, a scratch exception is acquired, i is reset to 0, and the second pass begins over the original list, building optimized_image.

The crop pass. For each output slot it clones the current frame, and when disposals[i]==DelDispose it collapses a run of deleted frames while accumulating their display time, advancing both curr and i inside the inner while. It then crops prev_image to bounds[i] into bgnd_image, sets the slot's disposal, special-cases DupDispose (zero delay, NoneDispose), advances curr for non-dup slots, and appends. Note that the inner DelDispose loop advances curr and i without re-checking curr!=NULL, and the delay math divides by curr->ticks_per_second. After the loop both arrays are freed and the scratch exception destroyed.

If the crop loop broke early (curr not fully consumed, e.g. a clone/crop failure), the partial output is destroyed and NULL returned; otherwise the head of the optimized list is returned to the caller. This is the sole success exit.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Heap out-of-bounds write (CWE-787). The arrays hold 2N elements (indices 0..2N-1) only when add_frames. Each input frame that reaches this DupDispose finalization advances i by 2 (the i++ here plus the trailing i++ at line 400) and writes bounds[i+1]/disposals[i]. Because the branch taken is decided by attacker-controlled pixel comparisons (CompareImagesBounds/IsBoundsCleared) and geometry, an attacker can craft an animation where frames repeatedly select DupDispose (and DelDispose at 139-151 also grows i). Once i approaches 2N, bounds[i+1] at index up to 2N and disposals[i] write one or more elements past the allocation, corrupting the heap. The implementation must prove that i (and i+1) can never reach the element capacity for ANY sequence of branch outcomes; the parallel index/branch bookkeeping here does not obviously establish that bound, which is the central weakness of this routine.

NULL pointer dereference (CWE-476) and out-of-bounds read on disposals[]. The inner loop advances curr=GetNextImageInList(curr) and i without ever re-testing curr for NULL. If a trailing run of slots is marked DelDispose (which the first pass can emit at lines 147/357), curr can walk off the end of the list to NULL; the loop condition then re-reads disposals[i] (now possibly past the array) and, if still DelDispose, the body dereferences curr->delay on a NULL curr. Even when the loop exits, line 423 unconditionally reads curr->delay after the loop, crashing if curr became NULL. The implementation must guarantee curr!=NULL before every curr->delay and that i stays in range while scanning the DelDispose run.

Divide-by-zero (CWE-369) and integer overflow (CWE-190) in the delay computation. curr->ticks_per_second comes straight from the decoded image; a crafted frame with ticks_per_second==0 triggers an integer division by zero (SIGFPE / crash). Independently, curr->delay*1000 is computed in the frame's integer type before division and can overflow for large delay values, and time (size_t) accumulates across a long DelDispose run; the later prev_image->delay = time*prev_image->ticks_per_second/1000 can likewise overflow. The implementation must reject or clamp ticks_per_second==0 and use overflow-safe arithmetic on attacker-controlled delay/ticks values.

Inconsistent/under-sized allocation basis (CWE-131). bounds is sized from GetImageListLength(curr) and disposals from GetImageListLength(image). Here curr==GetFirstImageInList(image) so the lengths match, but the two arrays are indexed in lock-step by the same i throughout; any future change (or a caller passing image mid-list vs. the head) that makes these lengths diverge would leave one array shorter than the index range and produce an OOB write. Moreover the 2x factor is only a heuristic upper bound on synthetic frames — the routine relies on it being sufficient rather than checking i against the real capacity. The implementation must size both arrays from the same authoritative frame count and bounds-check i against it.

Object-lifetime / use-after-free hazard (CWE-416). prev_image, dup_image, and bgnd_image are manually reclaimed on a large matrix of data-dependent branches. If any path fails to null a handle after DestroyImage, or takes the DupDispose branch (which sets disposals[i-1]=DupDispose that is re-tested at line 335 to DestroyImage(dup_image)) such that dup_image is destroyed twice, a stale pointer can be freed or dereferenced later. The DupDispose path also DestroyImages prev_image and adopts dup_image; a mismatch between the disposal marker written and the object actually retained produces a use-after-free when the next iteration or the crop pass touches prev_image/bounds[i]. The implementation must maintain a strict invariant that each of the three handles is either owned-and-live or NULL at every branch join.

Untrusted crop rectangle (CWE-125/CWE-20). bounds[i] was assembled from attacker-controlled geometry and, on the BackgroundDispose expansion path (lines 260-285), through signed additions/subtractions of x/width/y/height that can yield a rectangle whose origin is negative or whose extent exceeds the frame. CropImage must itself clamp the RectangleInfo to the image extent; if bounds[i] were ever produced with an out-of-range width/height (e.g. via overflow in the width*height area math or the expansion arithmetic) and CropImage trusted it, a read/write outside the pixel cache could result. The implementation must confirm CropImage validates &bounds[i] against prev_image's real dimensions.

summary: OptimizeLayerFrames drives two parallel heap arrays (bounds[], disposals[]) with a single running index i that is advanced by an attacker-influenced amount (0, 1, or 2) per input frame, while the arrays are sized to only 2x the frame count. The dominant risk is a heap out-of-bounds write when the DupDispose/DelDispose synthetic-frame paths push i (and i+1) past the allocation; secondary risks are a NULL-deref / OOB read in the DelDispose collapse loop and a divide-by-zero on attacker-supplied ticks_per_second in the second pass, plus subtle image-lifetime (UAF) obligations across the disposal-finalization branches.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: OptimizeLayerFrames(const Image *image, const LayerMethod method, ExceptionInfo *exception) signature
reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point; the untrusted surface is the image list it receives, covered by the field/array anchors below.
Signed index advanced by a data-dependent amount and used to write bounds[i]/bounds[i+1]/disposals[i]; central to the OOB-write contract.
Flag; its only security relevance is that it doubles the allocation and enables the synthetic-frame paths, captured under the array and index findings.
Fixed capacity (N or 2N) paired with an index that can overrun; also inconsistent length basis vs disposals.
Parallel array indexed by the same i; sized from image while bounds is sized from curr — divergence would under-size one array.
Writes one past the current slot and advances i by 2 for one input frame — the concrete OOB-write primitive.
Second index-growth path emitting DelDispose markers that drive the fragile second-pass loop.
Unconditional write at index i after i may have been advanced twice; participates in the OOB-write set.
Advances curr/i without NULL/bounds re-check — NULL deref and OOB read.
Divide-by-zero when ticks_per_second==0 and integer overflow on delay/time; attacker-controlled metadata.
Complex branch matrix of frees and adoptions; a mismatch yields use-after-free/double-free.
Consumes an attacker-derived rectangle possibly widened by signed expansion math; relies on CropImage clamping.
Validates geometry equality and throws on mismatch; a guard, not a vulnerable sink.
Debug-only assertions, compiled out under NDEBUG; document expectations, no runtime enforcement to attack.
Metadata copy with an author note; no direct memory-safety contract beyond the lifetime handling already flagged.
External helpers not shown; they produce the RectangleInfo values but the risk lives at the array-index and crop sinks here, not in these signatures.
Signed width/height/x/y merges feed bounds[i]; the resulting-rectangle risk is captured under the CropImage finding rather than duplicated.
Error-path frees are correctly paired with returns; no leak or double-free evident in the shown paths.
Success exit; ownership transferred to caller, no issue.

findings.title: Heap out-of-bounds write on bounds[]/disposals[] via attacker-driven index overrun
findings.bug_class: Heap out-of-bounds write (running index exceeds allocation)
findings.invariant: For every possible sequence of per-frame branch outcomes, the index i (and i+1) written into bounds[] and disposals[] must remain strictly less than the number of elements allocated (N, or 2N when add_frames). The relationship between input-frame count and slots consumed must be provably bounded by the allocation.
findings.failure_mode: The DupDispose finalization advances i by 2 for a single input frame (this i++ plus the trailing i++ at line 400) and writes bounds[i+1]; the DelDispose path at lines 147-150 also grows i. An attacker crafts an animation whose per-frame pixel comparisons repeatedly select these synthetic-frame branches, so i approaches 2N and bounds[i+1]/disposals[i] write past the 2N-element allocation, corrupting adjacent heap memory. Impact: heap corruption leading to crash or potential code execution when processing a malicious multi-frame image with -layers optimize (OptimizePlusLayer).
findings.what_to_confirm: Trace the maximum value i can reach across all branch combinations and confirm it (and i+1 at line 355) is always < GetImageListLength * (add_frames?2:1); check whether any explicit bound on i vs allocation exists.

findings.title: NULL-pointer dereference / OOB read in DelDispose collapse loop
findings.anchor: while ( disposals[i] == DelDispose ) { time += curr->delay*1000/curr->ticks_per_second; curr=GetNextImageInList(curr); i++; }
findings.bug_class: NULL pointer dereference and out-of-bounds array read
findings.invariant: curr must be non-NULL before every curr->delay dereference, and i must stay within the disposals[] allocation while scanning a DelDispose run.
findings.failure_mode: The inner loop advances curr=GetNextImageInList(curr) and i without re-testing curr for NULL. If the final slots carry DelDispose markers, curr walks off the end of the list to NULL; the loop condition re-reads disposals[i] (potentially past the array, CWE-125) and, if still DelDispose, curr->delay dereferences NULL. Even on normal exit, line 423 unconditionally reads curr->delay, crashing when curr became NULL at the list end. Impact: denial of service via crafted animation.
findings.what_to_confirm: Determine whether a trailing DelDispose run is possible and whether curr can reach NULL while disposals[i] is still DelDispose or before line 423 executes.

findings.title: Divide-by-zero and integer overflow in frame-delay computation
findings.bug_class: Divide-by-zero / integer overflow on untrusted metadata
findings.invariant: curr->ticks_per_second must be non-zero before use as a divisor, and delay*1000 plus the accumulated time must not overflow their integer types.
findings.failure_mode: ticks_per_second is decoded from the input image; a frame with ticks_per_second==0 causes an integer division by zero (SIGFPE / crash) at lines 419 and 423. Separately, curr->delay*1000 is evaluated in the frame's integer type and can overflow for a large delay, and the size_t accumulator time can grow across a long DelDispose run, so the later prev_image->delay = time*prev_image->ticks_per_second/1000 can overflow to a wrong or negative delay. Impact: crash, or corrupted timing metadata in the output.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check whether the decoders guarantee ticks_per_second>=1 for every frame reaching here, and whether delay/time arithmetic is overflow-checked.

findings.title: Use-after-free / double-free risk in image-handle lifetime across disposal finalization
findings.anchor: prev_image=DestroyImage(prev_image); prev_image=dup_image, dup_image=(Image *) NULL; ... if ( disposals[i-1] == DupDispose ) dup_image=DestroyImage(dup_image);
findings.invariant: At every branch join, each of prev_image/dup_image/bgnd_image must be either a uniquely-owned live object or NULL; the disposal marker written must match the object actually retained so the next iteration and the crop pass never touch a freed handle.
findings.failure_mode: The finalization is a matrix of data-dependent DestroyImage/ReferenceImage/adoption operations. If the disposals[i-1]==DupDispose test at line 335 destroys dup_image after this branch already adopted dup_image into prev_image (or fails to null a handle after DestroyImage), a stale pointer is later freed or dereferenced. Because the selected branch depends on attacker-controlled pixel comparisons, a crafted sequence can steer the code into an inconsistent ownership state. Impact: use-after-free / double-free — potential crash or exploitable corruption.
findings.what_to_confirm: Enumerate ownership of prev_image/dup_image/bgnd_image on each exit of the finalization block and confirm no handle is freed twice or used after DestroyImage, especially the DupDispose interaction between lines 335-336 and 349-359.

findings.title: Crop uses an attacker-derived rectangle widened by signed expansion arithmetic
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read via unvalidated crop geometry
findings.invariant: CropImage must clamp the supplied RectangleInfo to the source image's real columns/rows; the width/height/x/y assembled here must not describe a region outside the pixel cache.
findings.failure_mode: bounds[i] is built from attacker-controlled geometry and, on the BackgroundDispose expansion path (lines 260-285), through signed additions/subtractions of x/width/y/height that can produce a negative origin or an extent larger than the frame. If CropImage trusted such a rectangle, it could read outside the source pixel cache. The area math (width*height) at lines 168/202-204/326-330 can also overflow, distorting which branch is taken. Impact depends on CropImage's own validation; latent because the bug would live in CropImage.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm CropImage validates/clamps &bounds[i] against prev_image dimensions and rejects negative or oversized rectangles.

audit_checklist: Prove the maximum reachable value of i (and i+1 at line 355) is strictly less than the element capacity GetImageListLength*(add_frames?2:1) for every sequence of DupDispose/DelDispose/None/Background/Previous branch outcomes.
Size bounds[] and disposals[] from the same authoritative frame count (both use curr or both use image) and add an explicit bounds check on i before each array write.
Guard the DelDispose collapse loop (418-422) so curr is re-checked for NULL before every curr->delay and i cannot read disposals[] past its end.
Reject or clamp curr->ticks_per_second==0 before the divisions at lines 419 and 423, and use overflow-safe arithmetic for delay*1000 and the accumulated time.

Audit prev_image/dup_image/bgnd_image ownership at every branch of the finalization block (335-382) for double-free / use-after-free, especially the DupDispose interaction between lines 335-336 and 349-359.
Confirm CropImage clamps &bounds[i] to the source image extent and that the bgnd_bounds expansion math (260-285) and width*height area products cannot produce an out-of-range rectangle.
